Medicaid data from across the United States of America was extracted to include patients between age 3 to 15, between 2001 to 2013. ICD codes were used to determine the diagnoses of interest.

The groups were:
-	134,388 healthy children who underwent appendicectomy; matched with healthy controls 1:5 (671,940 controls)
-	154,887 healthy children admitted for pneumonia, cellulitis or gastroenteritis; matched with healthy controls 1:5 (774,435 controls)

Each cohort was analysed for the primary outcome compared with its matched controls.

Interpretation:
This is a data-driven study, where some of its limitations are partially overcome by the sheer number of participants. This helps it achieve its impressive P values, which indicates that the appendicectomy and medical groups are likely to be truly different from the matched controls in terms of neurobehavioural diagnoses. While many confounders may give rise to this difference, this paper does not need to exclude these – it only aims to show that the cohorts’ dissimilarity in exposure to anaesthesia is not matched by a dissimilarity in primary outcome.

Interestingly, both appendicectomy and medical groups showed higher rates of dental or ophthalmology care compared with their matched controls. These outcomes were intentionally chosen to have no plausible link to anaesthesia, and the result suggests that some other factor (perhaps a propensity to higher healthcare utilisation) is responsible for the observed increased risk of neurobehavioural diagnosis for both cohorts.

Take-home message:
This study uses an enormous dataset to demonstrate that if anaesthesia exposure does influence neurobehavioural outcomes, its impact is far overshadowed by other factors – such as hospital admission.

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - NZ edition
fields.content_heading: Check out the newly refreshed Journal Watch
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

This edition of Journal Watch launches our new website, which features an updated look and a mobile-friendly browsing experience.

Journal Watch reviewers from Auckland in New Zealand have put together a selection of articles looking at novel approaches to preventing adverse events in ENT surgery, and the effectiveness of modern tools in airway management and vascular access.

Thanks as always to our reviewers who volunteer their expertise to provide a trans-Tasman take on the paediatric anaesthesia literature. 


Kind regards,

Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, Journal Watch subcommittee

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - July and August Edition
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

For this issue, reviewers from the Perth Children's Hospital have put together their take on the paediatric anaesthesia literature in July and August. Articles in this edition cover patient blood management, fasting, and emergence delirium - and more besides.

Thanks as always to our reviewers who volunteer their expertise to dissect what's happening in the paediatric anaesthesia literature. 


Kind regards,



Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, Journal Watch subcommittee


PS - We'd love to hear your feedback! Let us know your thoughts on Journal Watch by replying to this email.

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - December 2023
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,


Welcome to the last Journal Watch edition for 2023!


For this issue, reviewers from the Royal Children's Hospital and Monash Children's Hospital in Melbourne have put together their take on the paediatric anaesthesia literature in September and October 2023. 

Articles in this edition cover efforts to reduce the environmental impact of anaesthesia, reviews of anaesthetic techniques and devices, and a look at quality and neurodevelopmental outcomes in neonatal anaesthesia.

Thanks as always to our reviewers who volunteer their expertise to dissect what's happening in the paediatric anaesthesia literature. 


Kind regards,



Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, Journal Watch subcommittee


PS - We'd love to hear your feedback! Let us know your thoughts on Journal Watch by replying to this email.

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - June 2024
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

Welcome to another edition of Journal Watch!

Our periodical newsletter aims to give you a local perspective on the paediatric anaesthesia literature for the past few months, and is put together by anaesthetists from around Australia and New Zealand

For this issue, contributors from Queensland Children's Hospital and Townsville Hospital have reviewed articles covering obesity in children, the future of nitrous oxide, and novel uses of BIS, ultrasound and augmented reality in anaesthetic practice.

Thanks as always to our reviewers who volunteer their time and expertise to provide a perspective on what's happening in the specialty.

Kind regards,

Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, Journal Watch subcommittee

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - August 2024
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

Welcome to another edition of Journal Watch!

Our periodical newsletter aims to give you a local perspective on the paediatric anaesthesia literature for the past few months, and is put together by anaesthetists from around Australia and New Zealand

For this issue, contributors from Starship Children's Hospital in Auckland have covered techniques in paediatric neuraxial anaesthesia, techniques for managing challenging ventilation, and more.

We hope you enjoy this edition, and are grateful to our reviewers for lending their time and expertise to explore a local interpretation of the literature. Please feel free to get in touch by reply email with any comments or suggestions.

Kind regards,

Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, SPANZA Journal Watch subcommittee

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - September 2024
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

Hot on the heels of our last edition, we're pleased to bring you the latest Journal Watch update from Perth.

Our periodical newsletter aims to give you a local perspective on the paediatric anaesthesia literature for the past few months, and is put together by anaesthetists from around Australia and New Zealand

For this issue, contributors from Perth Children's Hospital have put together a review of the paediatric component of NAP7, a comparison of propofol and sevoflurane and postoperative pain, and more besides.

We hope you enjoy this edition, and are grateful to our reviewers for lending their time and expertise to explore a local interpretation of the literature. Please feel free to get in touch by reply email with any comments or suggestions.

Kind regards,

Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, SPANZA Journal Watch subcommittee

fields.subject: SPANZA Journal Watch - November 2024
fields.content:
Dear SPANZA members and Journal Watch subscribers,

We're pleased to bring you another Journal Watch edition - this time from Monash Children's Hospital and the Royal Children's Hospital in Melbourne.

This issue takes a look at high flow oxygenation, anaesthesia and neurodevelopment, Māori and First Nations young people, and more besides.

We hope you enjoy this edition, and are grateful to our reviewers for lending their time and expertise to explore a local interpretation of the literature. Please feel free to get in touch by reply email with any comments or suggestions.

Kind regards,

Dr Eamonn Upperton
Chair, SPANZA Journal Watch subcommittee
